summary: The article stays within the evidence by making bounded descriptive claims and not asserting unsupported causal explanations.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Only 466 of the 10,000 scanned repositories, or 5%, contained at least one of the four AI configuration file formats examined.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a prevalence result within a 10,000-repository sample and only for four scanned file types; it does not explain why adoption was low or establish prevalence for repository-native agent guidance more broadly.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Among repositories that used an AI configuration file, TypeScript repositories were the largest language group, while adoption was spread across several other languages at lower counts.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a distribution pattern across languages, not a causal account of why TypeScript leads or whether the pattern reflects the broader repository population.

bounded_claims.claim_text: AGENTS.md files did not converge on a consistent structure, even though conventions or best practices, contribution guidance, and architecture or project structure were the most common heading categories.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a bounded structural claim for a filtered AGENTS.md subset and only from level 1 and level 2 heading analysis; it does not justify stronger claims about all agent-instruction documents or about the causes of structural variation.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Most AGENTS.md files in the analyzed set were either never revised after creation or changed only once, while the high-change subset was dominated by added and modified instructions.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports an observed maintenance split within the studied files, but the detailed change-category pattern comes from a narrow high-change subset of 10 files and does not establish how all AGENTS.md files evolve over time.
